text: The surgeon achieves exposure to the vesicles, noting the large size of the vesicle observed in resonance imaging. The surgeon holds the deferent and performs a medial dissection using a vascular plane to reach the final vesicle.
The surgeon avoids using cautery while navigating the complex contact between the vesicle axis and gastric structures, preparing to equip the vascular pedicle with assistance.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the left seminal vesicle and holds the right deferent to develop the medial plane for the seminal vesicle. The large seminal vesicle is identified before connecting the deferent. The fourth robotic arm is used to reposition and expose the large vesicle, avoiding cautery during dissection. The surgeon clips the vesicle, emphasizing the importance of traction and exposure for successful removal.
The surgeon clips the final vesicle pedicle artery, visible in the surgical field, while addressing a stain on the camera lens.

The surgeon carefully dissects below the dorsal venous plexus to preserve its fibers and minimize bleeding. The dissection is performed in a retrograde manner, connecting the lateral and posterior planes while avoiding opening the veins to maintain an avascular approach.

The surgeon attempts to gain more space by dissecting under the dorsal venous plexus, carefully avoiding significant bleeding. The pedicle is visible on the right, where the PI-REDS-IV lesion is located. A margin of fat is intentionally left at the right base to prevent a positive margin due to the presence of disease. The surgeon works to connect the dissection planes at the apex, which are closely adhered.

The surgeon has connected the lateral and posterior dissection planes, completing the dissection except for the prostate pedicle. A larger margin is left on the right side due to the presence of disease, with the closed area preserved around the vascular pedicle.

The surgeon attempts to bind the median pedicle using a brush, while maneuvering under the cleft to visualize both sides. The tweezers initially used are ineffective, prompting a switch to a more suitable instrument for handling the anterior aspect. Significant bleeding is observed during this process.

On the left side, the surgeon attempts to rotate the arm but lacks the angle, opting for a more aggressive clamp to apply pressure on the back. The surgeon identifies the duplex and carefully maneuvers under the dorsal venous plexus to preserve it.

The surgeon carefully identifies a low plane on the left side, entering under the plexus vein to preserve the veins while avoiding significant disease areas. The veins are gently pushed aside during this delicate part of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon gradually releases the dorsal venous plexus, preserving its upward orientation. Attempts are made to rotate the clamp on the left side, encountering difficulty due to the clamp's design.

The surgeon adjusts the tweezers to improve grip and releases some tension on the dorsal plexus. They search for and identify a vascular plane between the folds of the plexus, bringing it into closer view.
The surgeon performs a retrograde dissection on the left side, which appears more loose due to the absence of significant disease. The tissue is carefully manipulated to reveal anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the neurovascular tissue while preserving the adjacent plexus. The dissection plane is clearly defined between the plexus and prostate, minimizing bleeding. The surrounding tissue, including the retina, is fully exposed.
The surgeon delineates the urethra while releasing the flexion and prostatic axis on the right side, ensuring the area is completely relaxed.

The surgeon identifies a vesicle polyp and clips the associated artery to prevent bleeding. Bladder fibers are visible around the area. The prostate is controlled, and the liver plan is completely released, indicating the location for pedicle clipping while preserving the muscular liver.
The surgeon clips the base of the prostate, which is adhered to surrounding tissue, and releases it slightly to facilitate further dissection and removal.

The surgeon places a suture on the vesicle collar to close it slightly, noting that the right side is more adhered due to a greater volume of disease at the right base. The surgeon uses a Stratafix or Quill suture, which is their preferred choice for this procedure.

The surgeon performs posterior reconstruction using a thread with two needles, focusing on the cutting edge of the anastomosis. A point is placed behind the perineal traction area, which can be removed later. The reconstruction involves careful placement of sutures to reduce tension on the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a posterior suture on the back of the skin and along the edge of the dendroplasia, bringing the tissues closer together. The surgical field is observed without aspiration, and the surgeon manually clears the area to improve visibility.

The surgeon uses a second line of suture to perform a colovesical anastomosis, carefully suturing the posterior urethra to the bladder. The bladder is visibly brought closer to the rectum during this process.

The surgeon performs the posterior anastomosis using scaled wires, which visibly draw closer together. The posterior reconstruction is completed, with tension noted in the area, but the anastomosis will be adjusted to alleviate this tension.

The surgeon ties two wires together, one from the bottom and one from the posterior reconstruction, to approximate the tissues. The process requires careful attention as the piece does not easily align, indicating potential difficulty in achieving precise approximation.
